passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions
passages.heading: Categories, functions, and constructions
passages.text:
why a name is carried

by many at once,
[0.5em]
why we don't just have

our own to carry.Laurie D. Graham, Calling It Back to Me

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Box: Learning objectives
passages.heading: Learning objectives
passages.text:
- Analyze complex subject properties including position, categories, denotation, and licensing constraints

- Understand subject dropping patterns in English speech and distinguish grammatical from ungrammatical omissions

- Identify determiner functions and distinguish between determinatives (category) and determiners (function)

- Recognize different types of objects and apply passive alternation tests to identify them

- Distinguish complements from modifiers in NPs and AdjPs, understanding their selection and licensing

- Analyze modifier types, positions, stacking patterns, and integrated vs supplementary distinction

- Recognize major English constructions (resultative, light verb, conditional coordination, etc.) and their form-meaning relationships

- Apply diagnostic tests to identify syntactic functions and categorize complex grammatical relationships

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions
passages.heading: Categories, functions, and constructions
passages.text:
Tests used in this chapter
This chapter introduces several diagnostic techniques for analyzing categories, functions, and constructions:

- Subject-verb agreement test: Change number/person of potential subjects to see which controls verb agreement

- Passive alternation test: Check if objects can become subjects in passive constructions: They reported a theft A theft was reported

- Pronoun case test: Verify subjects use nominative case (I, he, she) while objects use accusative (me, him, her)

- Determiner ordering test: Check if elements can appear in of the NOUN pattern (some of the people, many of the changes)

- Complement vs modifier test: Assess optionality, selection by head, and ability to stack multiple instances

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects
passages.heading: Subjects
passages.text:
In everyday discourse, the term subject refers to topics of discussion, areas of study, focal points in visual arts, and even roles in sociopolitical contexts. In syntax, though, subject has a specialized technical meaning denoting one kind of function of a phrase (remember that one-word phrases are common), a special sub-type of complement.

There are no perfect definitions (see Section ), but we can provide a set of characteristics.

While Section introduced the basic syntactic roles and behaviors of subjects, here we'll consider some exceptions and variations. The subject function in a clause typically exhibits the following characteristics:

- The subject is a phrase (often an NP, but can also be a clause, VP, or even PP) that occurs before the head verb in a standard declarative clause.

- It appears after an auxiliary verb in most interrogative clauses.

- Semantically, the subject often denotes the agent/actor in active clauses, but can also represent the patient/recipient in passive constructions or an instrument or other role. (A patient is the entity that undergoes an action or is affected by it, as opposed to the agent who performs the action. For example, in The dog chased the cat, the cat is the patient.)

- It's omitted or found in the by-phrase of any related passive clause.

- If the subject is a pronoun, it's typically in the nominative case (see Table ).

- The subject is licensed by the head verb, with different verbs allowing different types of subjects based on their semantic properties.

- It usually controls the person and number agreement of a present-tense verb, though features like collective vs plural interpretation can impact agreement patterns.

To illustrate these characteristics more concretely, consider the following examples.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Position of subjects relative to verbs
passages.heading: Position of subjects relative to verbs
path: Position of subjects relative to verbs
passages.text:
In each example, the subject is italicized and its verb is in small caps.

[]They greeted us.

[]The cat sleeps on the porch.

[]Quickly, I need to go to the store.

[]Did you meet him at noon?

[]Guys, can you help me?

[]If it works, I'll be pleased.

Consider, though, the following constructions (discussed in Section ).

[]Up popped the toast

[]In the pond live thousands of tadpoles.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Categories of subjects
passages.heading: Categories of subjects
passages.text:
In the examples above, all the subjects are regular NPs. Here are some others.

[]Whether it does is unclear.[Clause]

[]Here, to believe wholeheartedly is not always a virtue.[Infinitival VP]

[]Studying grammar seems useful.[Participial VP]

[]What you know can help.[Relative NP]

[]After 2:00 is too late.[PP]

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Denotation
passages.heading: Denotation
passages.text:
Although the (syntactic) subject is typically the (semantic) topic, it needn't be. We can nominate other topics with phrases such as when it comes to, as for, as far as goes, speaking of, etc., in which cases, the topic of the sentence would not be its subject unless they happen to refer to the same thing (e.g., As for me, I hadn't thought about it).

Subjects denoting actors/agents:

[]As for the rest, I'll take them with me.

[]Who cooked dinner?

Subjects denoting patients/recipients:

[]The ball was hit over the fence, and the window got smashed.

[]We were given a choice.

Subjects denoting instruments and other roles:

[]The computer helped me speed up the analysis.

[]Studying grammar seems useful.

[]That the stars are visible surprises me.

[]The broom fell over.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Active vs passive
passages.heading: Active vs passive
passages.text:
Not every active clause has a passive counterpart, but when one exists, the subject of the active clause is typically omitted in the passive, though it can appear in an optional by PP.

[]I dropped it. It was dropped.

[]We told them. They were told.

[]Anybody can use the room. The room can be used by anybody.

In (c), don't think that anybody remains the subject in the passive clause. Instead, the room becomes the subject, and anybody is the object in the by PP.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Pronoun case
passages.heading: Pronoun case
passages.text:
Pronoun subjects are overwhelmingly in the nominative case: I, you, he, she, we, they. See the examples above. In more formal participial constructions, the possessive may be used; the accusative is now commoner, but may sound too informal:

[]Their/Them asking usis a huge honor.

[]His/Him hiding itwas a problem.

[]Your/You saying sodoes not make it true.

In a to infinitival VP, the subject appears in the accusative case after for. It's not for me to say. It's time for us to go. I've been waiting for them to ask. Is there a way for him to try again?

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > How subjects are licensed
passages.heading: How subjects are licensed
passages.text:
The subject is a kind of complement licensed by the head verb, but instead of following the verb inside the VP, as most such complements do, it typically appears inside the clause but outside of the VP: Subject Head VP.

Verbs almost universally license NP subjects. The other types of subjects are sensitive to selection by the licensing verbs, and only a few verbs license those subjects.

A whether clause is licensed as the subject by only a few lexical verbs such as affect, bother, concern, depend, influence, interest, matter, and worry. This construction is also mildly polarity sensitive, preferring negative contexts (see ). Compare

[]Whether it is true or not doesn't worry me much.[negative]

[?]Whether it is true or not worries me.[positive]

[*]Whether it rained last night explains the flood.[unlicensed]

Weather verbs, along with be in weather contexts, license dummy it (see Section ).

[]It snows here most of the time.

[]It's raining.

[]It should be sunny tomorrow.

Verbs of appearance and existence license dummy there:

[]There's a pen in the drawer.

[]There seem to be three options.

[]There appeared a gossamer web.

[*]There disappeared a gossamer web.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > subject-verb agreement for number and person
passages.heading: subject-verb agreement for number and person
path: subject-verb agreement for number and person
passages.text:
Agreement is mainly an issue with present-tense verbs for third-person singular subjects. It also affects the various forms of be in both the present and past tenses.

[]I'm here.

[]We're here.

[]She's here.

[]You seem kind.

[]The changes work well.

[]Mia and Iman go to U of T.

If you're wondering whether a particular NP is the subject, try changing its number and person (see also Box ). For example, let's say you're wondering about the subject in of all the girls who I have loved,. You've decided it could be all the girls (third-person plural), who (third-person singular), or I (first-person singular). You can try the following: of the girl who I have loved (no change in agreement), of all the girls I have loved (no change in agreement), and of all the girls who he has loved (have changes to has). This is good evidence that the subject is I.

Unfortunately, it's not a perfect test. Other elements may control agreement. This can be some dependent in the subject, as in A number of people have arrived. Or with dummy there, it's the complement of the head verb: There was something on the floor. There were some things on the floor.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Dropping subjects
passages.heading: Dropping subjects
passages.text:
In many languages it is common, and even sometimes obligatory, to omit the subject. For instance, Italian examples like () lack a subject, but the verb form tells you that it's first-person plural.

Abbiamo iniziato a studiare la grammatica.

have-1st-pl started to study the grammar

'We started studying grammar.'

Japanese often omits a subject, and without either number or person marking on the verb, the interpretation is entirely contextual.

食べた

tabeta

eat-past

'(I) ate.' (or somebody else)

行きたい?

ikitai

go-pres-desirative

'Do (you) want to go?' (or somebody else)

English subjects, though, are generally considered to be obligatory. Certainly, in formal writing, every main clause (apart from imperatives) has a subject. But in speech, even if the verb doesn't tell you who the subject is, it's often clear that it's the speaker or the addressee. In such cases, it's common to drop the subject I in English too, as in ().

[]Hope not.

[]Think so.

[]Dunno.

[]Can't find my keys.

[]Woke up late, ran out the door, and missed the bus.

For the addressee, it's also common to drop everything before the nucleus (Pre and subject together) in interrogatives, as in ().

[]Feeling OK?

[]Ever been to Paris?

[]Know what I mean?

[]Want some coffee?

In fact, it's possible to drop just the auxiliary and use you in each example in ().

Even third-person examples can work if the context is right, as in ().

[]A: Where did Ying go? B: Went to the store.

[]A: Why isn't Patel playing today? B: Got injured during practice.

[]A: Did the kids finish their homework? B: Finished it ages ago.

Even in main clauses, third-person subjects are regularly dropped when the clause is the second or subsequent coordinate in a coordination, although these are arguably coordinated VPs. Whichever analysis you prefer, constructions like those in () are very common in English.

[]They turned around and they made their way back.

[]I opened it and I took a look.

[]A nurse came into the room and she asked Bill how he was doing

What this shows is that subject dropping is actually quite common when the subject is easily understood. In some languages, like Italian, it's easier because of verb endings. In others, like Japanese and Korean, it's just expected that the listener will follow the topic closely and work it out. There's more about this in Section.

It's common for English-language teachers to insist that their students produce "full sentences." This is misguided and can be confusing. It gives many students the idea that, to be polite, their English needs to sound stiff, unnatural, and disconnected. If they follow their teachers' admonishments, they may end up seeming more alien than if they used the drop forms.

That said, students commonly drop subjects where no expert English speaker would. This kind of subject dropping is ungrammatical and can be quite confusing to the audience. It should be discouraged when there is an appropriate opportunity for form-focused feedback.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Dropping subjects > Box: Check your understanding: subject properties
passages.heading: Check your understanding: subject properties
path: Box: Check your understanding: subject properties
passages.text:
Test your grasp of subject complexity:

- Can you explain why There were some books has some books controlling agreement, not there?

- In what contexts is subject dropping acceptable in English? When is it problematic?

- How would you help a student who says *Is very difficult instead of It's very difficult?

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Conclusion
passages.heading: Conclusion
passages.text: Subjects exhibit a range of characteristics, including their position relative to verbs, the categories that can function as subjects, their typical denotation, their behavior in active and passive constructions, and their role in subject-verb agreement. The topic of subject dropping highlights the differences between English and many other languages, while also showcasing the often-unacknowledged flexibility of subject omission in informal English speech. By examining these properties and considering counter-examples, we can better anticipate the kinds of problems our students will face.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function
passages.heading: Determiners as a syntactic function
passages.text:
It's time to flip things around. For this section, we'll start with a bunch of examples, and then it will be up to you to draw out the characteristics.

Before we start, though, a quick reminder: determinative, like adjective, is a lexical category. Determiner, like modifier, is a syntactic function.

Hardly a day goes by without progress.

I saw him only a few days ago.

We've only got a little time.

All good people are welcome.

Do you want any fresh coffee?

The other two rooms had windows at both ends.

It happened each year.

Most people work about eight hours a day.

My new job takes far less than 40 hours per week.

How many times have I walked down this street where you lived?

Many more changes are coming.

Neither definition seems correct.

There is almost no water left.

Give me that book, not this one.

I'm going to take these sandwiches for my lunch.

It's different for us women.

Which ones do you like?

Even your critics say you're right this time.

Canada's population is growing quickly.

He's head of the World Health Organization's AIDS program.

- In which phrasal category/ies are determiners dependents?

- Where do determiners appear relative to the head?

- Where do determiners appear relative to modifiers?

- How does number and countability impact the need for a determiner?

- What is the role of determiners regarding definiteness?

- What kind of agreement do we find for determiners?

- What are the phrase types that most commonly function as determiners?

Determiners: a brief history

The very idea that determiners are something distinct from modifiers wasn't recognized until about 1933, at least, not as it applies to English. In his seminal 1933 work Language, Leonard BloomfieldBloomfield, Leonard introduced the term determiner to refer to a specific syntactic function performed by what he called "limiting adjectives."

Bloomfield observed that certain noun expressions, such as house or big house, are always accompanied by a determiner (Bloomfield, 1984). Modifiers, in contrast, are optional. This is sort of like an anthropologist realizing that some of these ancient arrowheads they'd known about for hundreds of years were actually spear points.

Despite almost 100 years having passed since Bloomfield's recognition of determiner as a distinct syntactic function, most pedagogical grammars remain confused on the point. If they use the term, they apply it indistinctly both to the category of determinatives and the function of determiners. It doesn't really matter what terms you use, but it seems very useful to me to maintain terminological hygiene, which is to say not to use one term for two distinct concepts.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Position and ordering within NP
passages.heading: Position and ordering within NP
passages.text:
Determiners appear only in NPs, typically preceding all other dependents within the NP. For example:

- the big red ball

- some really interesting books

- every student in the class

There are cases where there is a predeterminer modifier, but these are rare and I've never been asked about them by ESL students. For completeness, it's worth mentioning that all functions as just such a predeterminer modifier in all the books, with the functioning as the determiner.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Categories that function as determiners
passages.heading: Categories that function as determiners
passages.text:
The determiner function is a determinative phrase (DP) in the vast majority of cases. The remaining cases are almost all possessive NPs, either pronouns like my, their, whose, etc. or NPs headed by common or proper nouns, such as the last two examples in ().

A possessive NP often includes its own determiner, as in the two examples in Figure. My uncle's dog has my as the determiner in the NP my uncle's, which, in turn, functions as the determiner in the larger NP. In this morning's headlines, singular this is the determiner in the singular NP this morning's, not in the plural NP *this headlines.

Figure: Tree diagrams showing nested determiner structures in my uncle's dog and this morning's headlines.

Beyond these possessive structures, certain interrogative and relative words - the determinatives which and what, and the pronoun whose - can also function as determiners (see Sections and ).

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Agreement and concord
passages.heading: Agreement and concord
passages.text: Determiners often exhibit agreement with the head noun in terms of number and, in some cases, gender. This phenomenon is known as concord.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Agreement and concord > Number agreement
passages.heading: Number agreement
passages.text:
Determiners typically agree with the head noun in terms of number:

- Singular: the book, this car, my friend

- Plural: the books, these cars, my friends

Some determiners, such as some, any, and no, can be used with both singular and plural nouns without any change in form.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Agreement and concord > Gender agreement
passages.heading: Gender agreement
passages.text:
In English, gender agreement is not as prevalent as in many other languages. Still, there are a few cases where determiners agree with the natural gender of the referent:

- Masculine: his book

- Feminine: her book

- Neuter: its book

It's important to note that gender agreement in English is primarily limited to personal pronouns and possessive determiners. Most determiners do not exhibit gender distinctions.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Objects as complements
passages.heading: Objects as complements
passages.text:
Objects are a special kind of complement in VPs and PPs. They're overwhelmingly NPs, and they differ from other types of VP complements in that the same NP can usually function as the subject in the passive construction, though not always as () shows.

[]Somebody reported a theft.[object]

[]A theft was reported.[subject in passive]

[]I weigh seventy kilograms.[object]

[*]Seventy kilograms is weighed by me.[subject in passive]

[]It became a problem.[non-object complement]

[*]A problem was become.[subject in passive]

Most objects in PPs can't undergo this same alternation, though it is sometimes possible.

[]She went to Toronto.[object in PP]

[*]Toronto was gone to.[subject in passive]

[]Someone broke into the house.[object in PP]

[]The house was broken into.[subject in passive]

Verbs and prepositions that take objects are called transitive, while those that don't are intransitive. Some verbs are ditransitive: they take two objects, the first being the indirect object, and the second being the direct object.

[]I gave them indirect the books direct.[two objects]

[]They were given the books.[indir obj becomes subj in passive]

[]The books were given to them.[dir obj becomes subj in passive]

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements
passages.heading: Other complements
passages.text: Section deals with complements in VPs and Section with complements in PPs, but other phrases also allow complements. Here we'll examine complementation in noun phrases and adjective phrases, which were only briefly addressed in Chapter.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in noun phrases
passages.heading: Complementation in noun phrases
passages.text: Noun phrases can take several types of complements, which differ from modifiers in being more tightly integrated with the head noun and often corresponding to arguments of related verbs.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in noun phrases > Preposition phrase complements
passages.heading: Preposition phrase complements
passages.text:
Many nouns take PP complements, often with specific prepositions. This is particularly common with deverbal nouns (nouns derived from verbs), where the PP complement often corresponds to the complement of the related verb:

[]her belief in ghosts[cf. believe in ghosts]

[]their reliance on fossil fuels[cf. rely on fossil fuels]

[]the attack on the fortress[cf. attack the fortress]

When the verb licenses a direct object, the related noun usually licenses an of PP.

[]management of the city[cf. manage the city]

[]the examination of evidence[cf. examine evidence]

[]their collection of artifacts[cf. collect artifacts]

Other nouns select PP complements unrelated to any verbal source:

[]a solution to the problem

[]access to healthcare

[]an addiction to sugar

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in noun phrases > Clausal and verb phrase complements
passages.heading: Clausal and verb phrase complements
passages.text:
Some nouns, particularly those related to speech and mental states, can take clausal complements:

[]the claim that Earth is flat

[]her suggestion that we leave early

Others, also related to speech and mental states, can take to-infinitival VP complements.

[]the desire to succeed

[]the decision to resign

Again, the type of complement often mirrors what's possible with the related verb:

[]She claimed that Earth is flat the claim that Earth is flat

[]They decided to resign the decision to resign

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in adjective phrases
passages.heading: Complementation in adjective phrases
passages.text: Adjectives can also take various types of complements, which differ from modifiers in being selected by specific adjectives.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in adjective phrases > Preposition phrase complements
passages.heading: Preposition phrase complements
passages.text:
Many adjectives select specific prepositions:

[]afraid of spiders

[]similar to his brother

[]proud of her achievements

[]dependent on fossil fuels

[]interested in linguistics

The choice of preposition is rarely predictable and needs to be learned with each adjective. Compare:

[]interested in grammar vs keen on grammar

[*]keen in grammar vs *interested on grammar

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in adjective phrases > Clausal and verb phrase complements
passages.heading: Clausal and verb phrase complements
passages.text:
Some adjectives can take clausal and VP complements. Again, these tend to be those related to speech or mental states:

[]happy that you came

[]certain that it would work

[]eager to help

[]busy preparing

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Conclusion
passages.heading: Conclusion
passages.text: While verb complementation may be more familiar to students, the complement system extends beyond VPs to other major phrasal categories. Both NPs and AdjPs employ a variety of complementation strategies to express relationships between heads and their arguments. The key distinction from modifiers lies in complements being selected by specific heads and often being obligatory for the phrase's grammaticality or complete meaning. Understanding these patterns helps explain both the syntactic structure of English phrases and the semantic relationships between their components.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers
passages.heading: Modifiers
passages.text:
Modifiers are dependents that augment, qualify, or restrict the meaning of their head. Unlike complements, they aren't licensed by specific heads and are generally optional. Let's look at some examples with the modifiers italicized:

[]The tall woman in red smiled broadly.

[]The surprisingly difficult exam we took stressed everyone quite badly.

[]She works very efficiently in the morning.

[]The old book on the shelf that I mentioned belongs to Pat.

Several key characteristics define modifiers:

- They're typically optional; removing them may affect meaning but usually preserves grammaticality (but consider poorly in The results compare poorly to last year's.)

- Multiple modifiers can stack up with the same head

- They can appear in various positions relative to the head

- Different phrasal categories can function as modifiers

- Unlike complements, you can usually add more

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Position of modifiers
passages.heading: Position of modifiers
passages.text:
The position of modifiers varies by the category of both the modifier and the phrase it's modifying. In NPs, we find both premodifiers (before the head) and postmodifiers (after the head):

[]two bright red cars[premodifiers]

[]the book on the table with the blue cover[postmodifiers]

[]the young student from Toronto[pre- and postmodifiers]

In clause structure, modifiers can generally appear in various positions:

[]Quickly, she answered the phone.[initial]

[]She quickly answered the phone.[pre-verbal]

[]She answered the phone quickly.[final]

Some modifiers have strong preferences for certain positions though. Compare:

[]The students merely wanted to help.

[?]The students wanted to help merely.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Categories functioning as modifiers
passages.heading: Categories functioning as modifiers
passages.text:
Different phrasal categories can function as modifiers:

- AdjPs typically function as modifiers in NPs: the happy child

- AdvPs can modify any type of phrase except NPs: very quickly

- PPs can function as modifiers in most types of phrases: the book on the shelf

- Relative clauses function mostly as modifiers in NPs: the book that I bought

- Even NPs can sometimes function as modifiers: It snowed last week, I met her this morning, an old folks' home, the faculty office.

- DPs can also function as modifiers: It wasn't all that difficult, We drank almost all the water.

Table: Phrase Types as Modifiers

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Categories functioning as modifiers > Measure phrases
passages.heading: Measure phrases
passages.text:
A number of prepositions and adjectives take measure-phrase modifiers:

[]two years after the change

[]six months in

[]three kilometres down the road

[]two feet into the air

[]two meters tall

[]three years old

[]six feet deep

[]ten centimeters wide

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Stacking and ordering
passages.heading: Stacking and ordering
passages.text:
Multiple modifiers can stack up with the same head, but their order isn't random. In NPs, premodifying adjectives tend to follow patterns based on their semantic type:

[]The lovely old brass lamp[opinion-age-material]

[?]The brass old lovely lamp[non-standard order]

Similarly, different types of modifiers in clause structure tend to follow certain ordering principles:

[]She usually works efficiently in the morning at the office.

[?]She usually works at the office efficiently in the morning.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Integrated vs supplementary modifiers
passages.heading: Integrated vs supplementary modifiers
passages.text:
A key distinction in modification is between integrated and supplementary modifiers. Integrated modifiers are part of the larger phrase:

[]The student who scored highest won a prize.[integrated]

[]The books on the top shelf need dusting.[integrated]

Supplementary modifiers are simply added to the larger utterance with little regard for phrase structure. They're typically set off in speech with a slight pause, and in writing with commas, parentheses, or dashes:

[]Pat, who scored highest, won a prize.[supplementary relative]

[]The books, dusty and neglected, need sorting.[supplementary AdjP]

[]The meeting, surprisingly, went well.[supplementary AdvP]

The distinction between integrated and supplementary modification has important structural consequences. Integrated modifiers can only appear where the phrase structure allows them: inside the phrase they're modifying. Supplementary modifiers, by contrast, can appear in various interpolated positions:

[]The professor, as everyone knew, had cancelled class.

[]The professor had, rather unexpectedly, cancelled class.

[]The professor had cancelled class, as it turned out.

By contrast, while integrated modifiers can stack up inside a single phrase, supplementary modifiers tend to appear one at a time, each adding a separate comment:

[]The tall young energetic teacher...[integrated stack]

[]The teacher, quite young, surprisingly energetic, arrived.
[supplementary series]

In terms of meaning, integrated modifiers help identify or characterize the meaning of their head, while supplementary modifiers add parenthetical information or speaker commentary. This difference is particularly clear with relative clauses:

[]Students who finish early may leave.[integrated - defines subset]

[]The students, who finished early, left.[supplementary - adds info]

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Integrated vs supplementary modifiers > Box: Pattern recognition: modifiers vs complements
passages.heading: Pattern recognition: modifiers vs complements
path: Box: Pattern recognition: modifiers vs complements
passages.text:
Notice the systematic differences that help distinguish modifiers from complements:

- Optionality: Modifiers can usually be removed (the tall student the student), while complements often can't (proud of her *proud)

- Selection: Complements are selected by specific heads (afraid OF, not *afraid TO), while modifiers are more flexible

- Stacking: Multiple modifiers can pile up (the tall young energetic teacher), but complements are typically limited

Why might this distinction matter for teaching grammar effectively?

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions
passages.heading: Constructions
passages.text:
Language is not just a collection of individual words, and it's not just the words that hold meaning. Consider phrases like come here, think about it, or don't touch that. The form conveys something more than the sum of the word meanings. Try to take a step back and see it in a new light.

First of all, did you notice that the phrases address 'you'. If English had evolved a little differently, perhaps think about it could mean 'we think about it', 'she thinks about it', or 'they think about it'. In fact, in Japanese, for instance, the closest structural translation, dropping the subject, often means 'I'll think about it'. But not in English.

For us, the word you is absent and yet the directive is aimed at the person being spoken to. It is the form - the grammatical structure itself - that says 'you', and even a slightly different structure (to think about it or thinks about it) means something different.

Secondly, I called it a directive, and it absolutely is. This may seem obvious, but the phrase means both 'I want you to do this' and, at the same time, means 'I believe that our social and contextual relationship is such that I can reasonably expect you to comply'. It includes an element of compulsion (see Section ).

The form of think about it didn't have to convey a directive. It's close to a request, but not quite. It could, perhaps, have been a declaration, an exclamation, or a question. It could even have been a conditional = 'if you think about it'.

This may seem rather implausible to you. You probably wonder how think about it could possibly be conditional, and find the notion utterly preposterous. But not only could it convey conditionality, it does when part of a larger construction: think about it and you'll see what I mean (= 'if you think about it, you'll see what I mean').

When linguists speak about constructions, this is what they have in mind: not just some kind of grammatical structure but one that means something, a form-meaning pairing.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Conditional coordination
passages.heading: Conditional coordination
passages.text:
One particularly striking example of a construction is the think about it example. I'll call this the conditional coordination construction.

[]go and you'll regret it

[]touch that and you'll burn yourself

What appears on the surface to be simple coordination with and actually expresses a conditional relationship: 'if you do X, then Y will happen.' The construction requires specific elements to work: the first coordinate must be an imperative and the second must express the predicted consequence. If we alter either requirement, the conditional meaning disappears:

[]Come closer and I'll tell you a secret.

[*]You came closer and I'll tell you a secret.

[]Come closer and tell me.[coordination only]

A closely related construction is what Peter Culicover (1970) called the one more construction, as in One more comment like that and I'm leaving. Again, the first coordinate can be interpreted conditionally, as 'if you make one more comment like that'.

The conditional-coordination construction is particularly useful for expressing warnings, threats, and promises. Like many constructions, it shows how English grammar can package complex meanings into conventional patterns that go well beyond the literal meanings of the words involved.

Another syntax-semantics mismatch

Culicover et al. (1997) made a beautiful observation about these constructions: while syntactically the structure appears to be coordination (and joining two equal clauses), semantically the first clause is actually subordinate to the second. This explains why go and you'll regret it means 'if you go, you'll regret it' rather than 'I direct you to go and subsequently you will regret it'. This syntax-semantics mismatch is yet one more example of how constructions can encode meaning patterns that diverge from what we might expect from their structure. It's also another reminder: believe in the fallacy of monosemy, and you'll regret it (see Section ).

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Resultative constructions
passages.heading: Resultative constructions
passages.text:
In a resultative construction, an NP object and a following phrase express the result of the action denoted by the verb:

[]She wiped the table clean.

[]He hammered the metal flat.

[]It froze the lake solid.

The result phrase is usually an AdjP (as above) but can sometimes be a PP:

[]She tied her hair into braids.

[]They smoothed the dough to perfection.

[]He carved the wood down to size.

Importantly, the object NP must be affected by the action: we can't say *She drove the road flat or *He sang the baby hoarse. The result must also be a plausible outcome of the action: *She painted the wall tall is odd because painting doesn't cause height change.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Light verb constructions
passages.heading: Light verb constructions
passages.text:
Light verb constructions combine a verb that is semantically near empty with an NP to express what could often be conveyed by a single verb:

[]take a walk ('walk')

[]have a look ('look')

[]give a try ('try')

[]make a decision ('decide')

When I say take, have, give, and make are semantically empty, I mean they appear more for their syntactic properties than for their meaning: Giving money and having money convey very different things, but give a try and have a try don't.

The most common light verbs are take, have, give, and make. While these constructions might seem redundant, they serve important discourse functions. Compare:

[]I walked in the park yesterday.

[]I took a nice long walk in the park yesterday.

The light verb construction allows some modification of the event (denoted by the noun walk here) more easily than does the simple verb. It also helps manage information structure, as the event noun can more easily serve as given information in subsequent discourse (See Chapter ).

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Serial verb constructions
passages.heading: Serial verb constructions
passages.text:
English has a limited but common set of serial-verb constructions where two verbs combine without any coordinator, subordinator, or preposition:

[]Go get some coffee.

[]Come see what I found.

[]Run grab your coat.

The first verb is typically a verb of motion (go, come, run) and the second describes the action to be performed after the motion. Both verbs must be in the same form: we can't mix forms like *went get or *go getting.

These constructions are more restricted in English than in many other languages. They're mainly limited to imperatives and infinitivals:

[]I'll go get some coffee.

[]She made me go get some coffee.

[*]I went got some coffee.

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Predicative complement constructions
passages.heading: Predicative complement constructions
passages.text:
These constructions involve a verb taking both an object and a complement that predicates something of that object:

[]They elected him president.

[]The news made her happy.

[]We found the proposal acceptable.

The complement can be an NP or AdjP, but crucially it's not modifying the verb; it's predicating something of the object. Compare:

[]They painted the door red.[predicate complement]

[]They painted the red door.[attributive modifier]

In (a), red describes the result of the painting. In (b), it simply identifies which door was painted.

Different verbs allow different types of predicative complements:

- Make, get: mainly AdjPs (made her angry, got him ready)

- Consider, find: both NPs and AdjPs (consider him a friend/friendly)

- Elect, name: mainly 'role' NPs with or without a determiner (elected her treasurer, named him the captain)

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Spray/load alternations
passages.heading: Spray/load alternations
passages.text:
Some verbs allow their arguments to appear in two different arrangements, with a subtle difference in meaning:

[]Kim sprayed paint on the wall.

[]Kim sprayed the wall with paint.

[]Pat loaded boxes onto the truck.

[]Pat loaded the truck with boxes.

The (a) variant emphasizes the motion of the substance, while the (b) with variant emphasizes the effect on the location. This often correlates with whether the location is completely affected: load the truck with boxes suggests the truck becomes full, while load boxes onto the truck implies no such thing.

Other verbs showing this pattern include pack, stuff, stack, pile, splash, scatter, and smear. Not all semantically similar verbs allow both patterns:

[]pour water into the glass / *pour the glass with water

[*]fill water into the glass / fill the glass with water

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Spray/load alternations > Box: Quick self-check: constructions
passages.heading: Quick self-check: constructions
passages.text:
Test your understanding of English constructions:

- What's the difference between Take a walk (light verb) and Walk (simple verb)?

- Why does Touch that and you'll regret it mean 'if you touch that'?

- Can you identify the result phrase in She hammered the metal flat?

passages.anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Summary
passages.heading: Summary
passages.text: Categories tell you what something is; functions tell you what it's doing; constructions tell you what the combination means. With those three lenses, you can pull apart any clause a student produces or any explanation a textbook offers. The subject diagnostics, the modifier-complement distinction, and the form-meaning pairings from this chapter give you the specific tests to back up your intuitions - or to discover that your intuitions were wrong.
